verses.text: As the LORD prepares to take Elijah, Elisha refuses to leave him, though urged at every stop. The sons of the prophets keep telling Elisha his master will be taken. He clings to the man of God to the last, unwilling to lose a moment of his presence.

As the LORD prepares to take Elijah, Elisha refuses to leave him, though urged at every stop. The sons of the prophets keep telling Elisha his master will be taken. He clings to the man of God to the last, unwilling to lose a moment of his presence.

As the LORD prepares to take Elijah, Elisha refuses to leave him, though urged at every stop. The sons of the prophets keep telling Elisha his master will be taken. He clings to the man of God to the last, unwilling to lose a moment of his presence.

Again and again Elijah says 'tarry here,' and again Elisha answers, 'I will not leave thee.' His devotion will not be shaken off. Those who would inherit a blessing must follow hard after it, refusing every excuse to settle for less than God's full gift.

Again and again Elijah says 'tarry here,' and again Elisha answers, 'I will not leave thee.' His devotion will not be shaken off. Those who would inherit a blessing must follow hard after it, refusing every excuse to settle for less than God's full gift.

Again and again Elijah says 'tarry here,' and again Elisha answers, 'I will not leave thee.' His devotion will not be shaken off. Those who would inherit a blessing must follow hard after it, refusing every excuse to settle for less than God's full gift.

At the Jordan, Elijah asks what he may do, and Elisha asks for 'a double portion of thy spirit' — the heir's share. Elijah calls it a hard thing, granted only if he sees him taken. Great spiritual gifts are sought boldly, yet given only on God's terms.

At the Jordan, Elijah asks what he may do, and Elisha asks for 'a double portion of thy spirit' — the heir's share. Elijah calls it a hard thing, granted only if he sees him taken. Great spiritual gifts are sought boldly, yet given only on God's terms.

A chariot of fire parts them, and Elijah goes up in a whirlwind; Elisha cries, 'My father, my father, the chariot of Israel.' Heaven's fire escorts the faithful prophet home. The true defence of a nation was not its armies but the man who walked with God.

A chariot of fire parts them, and Elijah goes up in a whirlwind; Elisha cries, 'My father, my father, the chariot of Israel.' Heaven's fire escorts the faithful prophet home. The true defence of a nation was not its armies but the man who walked with God.

Elisha takes up the fallen mantle, strikes the Jordan, and cries, 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' — and the waters part. The prayer is answered at once. God's power did not depart with Elijah; the same LORD stands ready for the servant who calls on Him.

Elisha takes up the fallen mantle, strikes the Jordan, and cries, 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' — and the waters part. The prayer is answered at once. God's power did not depart with Elijah; the same LORD stands ready for the servant who calls on Him.

The sons of the prophets bow to Elisha, yet insist on searching three days for Elijah, finding nothing. Their unbelief cannot accept the plain truth he told them. We waste much effort searching for what God has already made clear, when we will not simply believe.

Jericho's water is bad and the ground barren, so Elisha casts salt in the spring and heals it 'unto this day.' His first miracle is one of mercy, making bitter waters sweet. The God who takes His prophets to glory also heals the broken places they leave behind.

Youths from Bethel mock the prophet — 'Go up, thou bald head' — scorning God's servant and His taken prophet, and two bears maul them. The mockery was no harmless teasing but contempt for God. To despise His messengers is a graver thing than the scoffers suppose.
